question: How does the recipient model of a BPMN message event differ from that of a BPMN signal event in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: A message is delivered to a single recipient (1:1); a signal is delivered to multiple recipients (1:N).
A message is broadcast to all subscribers (1:N); a signal is targeted to one specific instance (1:1).
Both messages and signals are 1:N broadcasts; the only difference is the visual notation.
Both messages and signals are 1:1 deliveries, but signals additionally require a `correlationKey`.

a.text: Message events form a single sender to single recipient relationship (1:1); the message cannot have more than one recipient. Signal events form a single sender to several-recipient relationship (1:N).

b.text: Reversed. Messages are not broadcast; signals are. A signal triggers every process instance with a token waiting at a corresponding catch event, even across different processes.

c.text: The difference is semantic, not only visual. Messages target one recipient; signals reach every matching subscriber.

d.text: Correlation keys belong to the message world. Signals are broadcast by name and reach all matching subscribers, so the cardinality is genuinely different.

explanation: Message events are used when a process instance must wait for a message from a specific sender — a 1:1 relationship. Signal events are broadcasts; a single thrown signal triggers every catching subscriber across processes, giving a 1:N relationship.
